punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to two thousand and five hundred rupees, or with both
ingredients: voluntarily obstructs any public servant
in the discharge of his public functions
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 221, Page 62

description: Whoever, being bound by law to render or furnish assistance to any public servant in the execution of his public duty, intentionally omits to give such assistance,–– ( a ) shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to two thousand and five hundred rupees, or with both; ( b ) and where such assistance be demanded of him by a public servant legally competent to make such demand for the purposes of executing any process lawfully issued by a Court or of preventing the commission of an offence, or suppressing a riot, or affray, or of apprehending a person charged with or guilty of an offence, or of having escaped from lawful custody, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: A person legally required to assist a public servant who deliberately refuses to do so commits an offence; the punishment is up to one month imprisonment, or up to six months if the assistance is demanded for specific law‑enforcement purposes.
punishment: simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to two thousand and five hundred rupees, or with both; and where such assistance be demanded ... shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both
ingredients: bound by law to render or furnish assistance to any public servant in the execution of his public duty
intentionally omits to give such assistance
if assistance is demanded for execution of court process, prevention of offence, suppression of riot or affray, apprehension of accused, or escape from custody
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 222, Page 62

description: Whoever, knowing that, by an order promulgated by a public servant lawfully empowered to promulgate such order, he is directed to abstain from a certain act, or to take certain order with certain property in his possession or under his management, disobeys such direction,–– ( a ) shall, if such disobedience causes or tends to cause obstruction, annoyance or injury, or risk of obstruction, annoyance or injury, to any person lawfully employed, be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to two thousand and five hundred rupees, or with both; ( b ) and where such disobedience causes or tends to cause danger to human life, health or safety, or causes or tends to cause a riot or affray, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Disobeying a lawful order from a public servant is punishable; if the disobedience leads to obstruction or annoyance, the penalty is up to six months imprisonment, whereas if it endangers life or causes riot, the penalty rises to up to one year imprisonment.
punishment: simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to two thousand and five hundred rupees, or with both; and where such disobedience causes danger to life, health or safety or a riot, imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both
ingredients: knowing an order promulgated by a lawfully empowered public servant directing abstention or handling of property
disobeys such direction
disobedience causes or tends to cause obstruction, annoyance, injury, or risk thereof (sub‑section a)
disobedience causes or tends to cause danger to human life, health, safety, or a riot/affray (sub‑section b)
explanation: Explanation. —It is not necessary that the offender should intend to produce harm, or contemplate his disobedience as likely to produce harm. It is sufficient that he knows of the order which he disobeys, and that his disobedience produces, or is likely to produce, harm. Illustration. An order is promulgated by a public servant lawfully empowered to promulgate such order, directing that a religious procession shall not pass down a certain street. A knowingly disobeys the order, and thereby causes danger of riot. A has committed the offence defined in this section.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 223, Page 62

description: Whoever holds out any threat of injury to any public servant, or to any person in whom he believes that public servant to be interested, for the purpose of inducing that public servant to do any act, or to forbear or delay to do any act, connected with the exercise of the public functions of such public servant, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
summary: Making threats of injury to a public servant or someone connected with them, to compel the servant to act or refrain from acting in their official capacity, is punishable by up to two years imprisonment or a fine.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: holds out any threat of injury to any public servant or to a person in whom the public servant is interested
purpose is to induce the public servant to do, forbear, or delay any act connected with the exercise of his public functions
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 224, Page 63

description: Whoever holds out any threat of injury to any person for the purpose of inducing that person to refrain or desist from making a legal application for protection against any injury to
summary: A person who threatens injury to stop another from filing a legal protection application commits an offence; the statutory text is incomplete in this excerpt.
ingredients: holds out any threat of injury to any person
purpose is to induce that person to refrain or desist from making a legal application for protection against any injury to
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 225, Page 63

description: Whoever attempts to commit suicide with the intent to compel or restrain any public servant from discharging his official duty shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both, or with community service.
summary: A person who tries to kill himself to force or stop a public servant from performing official duties is punishable with up to one year imprisonment, fine, or community service.
punishment: simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both, or with community service
ingredients: attempt to commit suicide
intent to compel or restrain any public servant from discharging his official duty
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 226, Page 63

description: Whoever, being legally bound by an oath or by an express provision of law to state the truth, or being bound by law to make a declaration upon any subject, makes any statement which is false, and which he either knows or believes to be false or does not believe to be true, is said to give false evidence. Explanation 1.—A statement is within the meaning of this section, whether it is made verbally or otherwise. Explanation 2.—A false statement as to the belief of the person attesting is within the meaning of this section, and a person may be guilty of giving false evidence by stating that he believes a thing which he does not believe, as well as by stating that he knows a thing which he does not know. Illustrations. (a) A, in support of a just claim which B has against Z for one thousand rupees, falsely swears on a trial that he heard Z admit the justice of B’s claim. A has given false evidence. Threat of injury to public servant. Threat of injury to induce person to refrain from applying for protection to public servant. Attempt to commit suicide to compel or restrain exercise of lawful power. Giving false evidence.
summary: A person bound by oath or law who makes a false statement, knowing or believing it to be false, commits the offence of giving false evidence.
ingredients: legally bound by an oath or an express provision of law to state the truth
makes a statement which is false
knows or believes the statement to be false or does not believe it to be true
explanation: 1.—A statement is within the meaning of this section, whether it is made verbally or otherwise. 2.—A false statement as to the belief of the person attesting is within the meaning of this section, and a person may be guilty of giving false evidence by stating that he believes a thing which he does not believe, as well as by stating that he knows a thing which he does not know.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 227, Page 63

description: Whoever causes any circumstance to exist or makes any false entry in any book or record, or electronic record or makes any document or electronic record containing a false statement, intending that such circumstance, false entry or false statement may appear in evidence in a judicial proceeding, or in a proceeding taken by law before a public servant as such, or before an arbitrator, and that such circumstance, false entry or false statement, so appearing in evidence, may cause any person who in such proceeding is to form an opinion upon the evidence, to entertain an erroneous opinion touching any point material to the result of such proceeding is said “to fabricate false evidence”. Illustrations. (a) A puts jewels into a box belonging to Z, with the intention that they may be found in that box, and that this circumstance may cause Z to be convicted of theft. A has fabricated false evidence. (b) A makes a false entry in his shop-book for the purpose of using it as corroborative evidence in a Court. A has fabricated false evidence. (c) A, with the intention of causing Z to be convicted of a criminal conspiracy, writes a letter in imitation of Z’s handwriting, purporting to be addressed to an accomplice in such criminal conspiracy, and puts the letter in a place which he knows that the officers of the police are likely to search. A has fabricated false evidence.
summary: Creating a false circumstance, entry, or document with the intention that it be used as evidence and mislead a proceeding constitutes the offence of fabricating false evidence.
ingredients: causes any circumstance to exist or makes any false entry in any book, record, or electronic record, or makes any document containing a false statement
intending that such false evidence may appear in evidence in a judicial proceeding or before a public servant or arbitrator
intending that the false evidence may cause an erroneous opinion material to the result of the proceeding
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 228, Page 64

description: (1) Whoever intentionally gives false evidence in any stage of a judicial proceeding, or fabricates false evidence for the purpose of being used in any stage of a judicial proceeding, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine which may extend to ten thousand rupees. (2) Whoever intentionally gives or fabricates false evidence in any case other than that referred to in sub-section (1), shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine which may extend to five thousand rupees. Fabricating false evidence. Punishment for false evidence.
summary: Intentionally giving or fabricating false evidence in any stage of a judicial proceeding is punishable with up to seven years imprisonment and fine; a lesser penalty applies for other cases.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine which may extend to ten thousand rupees; imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine which may extend to five thousand rupees
ingredients: intentionally gives false evidence
intentionally fabricates false evidence
in any stage of a judicial proceeding
or in any case other than that referred to in sub-section (1)
explanation: 1.—A trial before a Court-martial is a judicial proceeding. 2.—An investigation directed by law preliminary to a proceeding before a Court, is a stage of a judicial proceeding, though that investigation may not take place before a Court. 3.—An investigation directed by a Court according to law, and conducted under the authority of a Court, is a stage of a judicial proceeding, though that investigation may not take place before a Court.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 229, Page 64

description: (1) Whoever gives or fabricates false evidence, intending thereby to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, any person to be convicted of an offence which is capital by the law for the time being in force in India shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine which may extend to fifty thousand rupees. (2) If an innocent person be convicte
summary: Providing false evidence with the intention or knowledge that it may lead to the conviction of a person for a capital offence is punishable with life imprisonment or up to ten years rigorous imprisonment and a fine.
punishment: imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine which may extend to fifty thousand rupees
ingredients: gives or fabricates false evidence
intending thereby to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, any person to be convicted of a capital offence
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 230, Page 65

description: Whoever gives or fabricates false evidence intending thereby to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, any person to be convicted of an offence which by the law for the time being in force in India is not capital, but punishable with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for a term of seven years or upwards, shall be punished as a person convicted of that offence would be liable to be punished. Illustration. A gives false evidence before a Court, intending thereby to cause Z to be convicted of a dacoity. The punishment of dacoity is imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, with or without fine. A, therefore, is liable to imprisonment for life or imprisonment, with or without fine.
summary: A person who gives or fabricates false evidence with the intention or knowledge that it will likely cause another person to be convicted of a non‑capital offence punishable with life imprisonment or seven years or more is liable to the same punishment as that offence. The section also provides an illustration involving false evidence leading to a dacoity conviction.
punishment: shall be punished as a person convicted of that offence would be liable to be punished.
ingredients: gives or fabricates false evidence
intends to cause or knows it is likely to cause conviction of a person
the offence for which conviction is sought is punishable with imprisonment for life or a term of seven years or upwards
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 231, Page 65

description: ( 1 ) Whoever threatens another with any injury to his person, reputation or property or to the person or reputation of any one in whom that person is interested, with intent to cause that person to give false evidence shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both. ( 2 ) If innocent person is convicted and sentenced in consequence of false evidence referred to in sub-section ( 1 ), with death or imprisonment for more than seven years, the person who threatens shall be punished with the same punishment and sentence in the same manner and to the same extent such innocent person is punished and sentenced.
summary: The section criminalises threatening a person with injury to compel them to give false evidence, prescribing imprisonment up to seven years or fine. If such threat leads to an innocent person’s conviction with death or imprisonment exceeding seven years, the threatener receives the same punishment as the innocent person.
punishment: shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both. If innocent person is convicted and sentenced in consequence of false evidence referred to in sub-section ( 1 ), with death or imprisonment for more than seven years, the person who threatens shall be punished with the same punishment and sentence in the same manner and to the same extent such innocent person is punished and sentenced.
ingredients: threatens another with injury to person, reputation or property
intent to cause that person to give false evidence
resulting conviction of an innocent person with death or imprisonment exceeding seven years
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 232, Page 65

description: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use as true or genuine evidence any evidence which he knows to be false or fabricated, shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave or fabricated false evidence.
summary: A person who knowingly uses or attempts to use false or fabricated evidence as genuine is punished as if he had given false evidence.
punishment: shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave or fabricated false evidence.
ingredients: corruptly uses or attempts to use as true evidence
knows the evidence is false or fabricated
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 233, Page 66

description: Whoever issues or signs any certificate required by law to be given or signed, or relating to any fact of which such certificate is by law admissible in evidence, knowing or believing that such certificate is false in any material point, shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
summary: Issuing or signing a legally required certificate while knowing or believing it to be materially false is punishable as giving false evidence.
punishment: shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
ingredients: issues or signs a certificate required by law
certificate is admissible in evidence
knowing or believing the certificate is false in any material point
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 234, Page 66

description: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use any such certificate as a true certificate, knowing the same to be false in any material point, shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
summary: Corruptly using or attempting to use a false certificate as genuine is punishable as giving false evidence.
punishment: shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
ingredients: corruptly uses or attempts to use a certificate as true
knowing the certificate is false in any material point
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 235, Page 66

description: Whoever, in any declaration made or subscribed by him, which declaration any Court or any public servant or other person, is bound or authorised by law to receive as evidence of any fact, makes any statement which is false, and which he either knows or believes to be false or does not believe to be true, touching any point material to the object for which the declaration is made or used, shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
summary: Making a false statement in a declaration that is legally receivable as evidence, with knowledge of its falsity, is punishable as giving false evidence.
punishment: shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
ingredients: makes a false statement in a declaration
declaration is receivable as evidence by a Court or public servant
knows or believes the statement to be false or does not believe it to be true
statement touches a material point to the object of the declaration
explanation: A declaration which is inadmissible merely upon the ground of some informality, is a declaration within the meaning of section 236 and this section.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 236, Page 66

description: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use as true any such declaration, knowing the same to be false in any material point, shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
summary: Corruptly using or attempting to use a false declaration as true is punishable as giving false evidence.
punishment: shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
ingredients: corruptly uses or attempts to use a declaration as true
knowing the declaration is false in any material point
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 237, Page 66

description: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, causes any evidence of the commission of that offence to disappear, with the intention of screening the offender from legal punishment, or with that intention gives any information respecting the offence which he knows or believes to be false shall,— ( a ) if the offence which he knows or believes to have been committed is punishable with death, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; ( b )   if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; ( c ) if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for any term not extending to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence, for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term of the imprisonment provided for the offence, or with fine, or with both. Illustration. A, knowing that B has murdered Z, assists B to hide the body with the intention of screening B from punishment. A is liable to imprisonment of either description for seven years, and also to fine.
summary: A person who, knowing an offence has been committed, either destroys evidence or provides false information to shield the offender faces imprisonment and possibly fine, with the severity depending on the original offence’s punishment.
punishment: if the offence which he knows or believes to have been committed is punishable with death, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for any term not extending to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence, for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term of the imprisonment provided for the offence, or with fine, or with both.
ingredients: knowing or having reason to believe an offence has been committed
causes evidence of the offence to disappear or gives false information with intention to screen the offender
punishment varies according to the nature of the offence (death, life, or term not exceeding ten years)
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 238, Page 66

description: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, intentionally omits to give any information respecting that offence which he is legally bound to give, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: A person who, aware of an offence, deliberately fails to provide information that he is legally required to disclose is punishable with up to six months’ imprisonment or a fine up to five thousand rupees.
punishment: shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
ingredients: knowing or having reason to believe an offence has been committed
intentionally omits to give any information respecting that offence
legally bound to give such information
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 239, Page 66

description: 240.  Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, gives any information respecting that offence which he knows or believes to be false, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. Explanation. —In sections 238 and 239 and in this section the word “offence” includes any act committed at any place out of India, which, if committed in India, would be punishable under any of the following sections, namely, 103, 105, 307, sub-sections ( 2 ), ( 3 ) and ( 4 ) of section 309, sub-sections ( 2 ), ( 3 ), ( 4 ) and ( 5 ) of section 310, 311, 312, clauses ( f ) and ( g ) of section 326, sub-sections ( 4 ), ( 6 ), ( 7 ) and ( 8 ) of section 331, clauses ( a ) and ( b ) of section 332.
summary: Provides punishment for anyone who knowingly gives false information about an offence, whether or not the offence occurred in India. Includes an explanation that the term “offence” covers acts committed outside India that would be punishable under specified sections if committed in India.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed
gives any information respecting that offence which he knows or believes to be false
explanation: In sections 238 and 239 and in this section the word “offence” includes any act committed at any place out of India, which, if committed in India, would be punishable under any of the following sections, namely, 103, 105, 307, sub-sections ( 2 ), ( 3 ) and ( 4 ) of section 309, sub-sections ( 2 ), ( 3 ), ( 4 ) and ( 5 ) of section 310, 311, 312, clauses ( f ) and ( g ) of section 326, sub-sections ( 4 ), ( 6 ), ( 7 ) and ( 8 ) of section 331, clauses ( a ) and ( b ) of section 332.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 240, Page 67

description: 241.  Whoever secretes or destroys any document or electronic record which he may be lawfully compelled to produce as evidence in a Court or in any proceeding lawfully held before a public servant, as such, or obliterates or renders illegible the whole or any part of such document or electronic record with the intention of preventing the same from being produced or used as evidence before such Court or public servant as aforesaid, or after he shall have been lawfully summoned or required to produce the same for that purpose, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Penalises anyone who destroys, conceals or renders illegible a document or electronic record that he is legally required to produce as evidence, with the intention of preventing its use in court or before a public servant.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both
ingredients: secretes or destroys any document or electronic record which he may be lawfully compelled to produce as evidence
intention of preventing the same from being produced or used as evidence
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 241, Page 67

description: 242.  Whoever falsely personates another, and in such assumed character makes any admission or statement, or confesses judgment, or causes any process to be issued or becomes bail or security, or does any other act in any suit or criminal prosecution, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
summary: Makes false personation of another person and, in that assumed identity, performs any act in a civil suit or criminal prosecution, such as making statements, confessing judgments, or obtaining bail.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: falsely personates another
makes any admission or statement, confesses judgment, causes any process to be issued, becomes bail or security, or does any other act in any suit or criminal prosecution
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 242, Page 67

description: 243.  Whoever fraudulently removes, conceals, transfers or delivers to any person any property or any interest therein, intending thereby to prevent that property or interest therein from being taken as a forfeiture or in satisfaction of a fine, under a sentence which has been pronounced, or which he knows to be likely to be pronounced, by a Court or other competent authority, or from being taken in execution of a decree or order which has been made, or which he knows to be likely to be made by a Court in a civil suit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Penalises fraudulent removal, concealment, transfer or delivery of property or interest with the intention of evading forfeiture, fine or execution of a decree.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both
ingredients: fraudulently removes, conceals, transfers or delivers any property or any interest therein
intending to prevent that property or interest from being taken as forfeiture, in satisfaction of a fine, or in execution of a decree or order
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 243, Page 67

description: 244.  Whoever fraudulently accepts, receives or claims any property or any interest therein, knowing that he has no right or rightful claim to such property or interest, or practises any deception touching any right to any property or any interest therein, intending thereby to prevent that property or interest therein from being taken as a forfeiture or in satisfaction of a fine, under a sentence which has been pronounced, or which he knows to be likely to be pronounced by a Court or other competent authority, or from being taken in execution of a decree or order which has been made, or which he knows to be likely to be made by a Court in a civil suit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
summary: Punishes anyone who fraudulently accepts, receives or claims property without a rightful claim, with the intention of evading forfeiture, fine or execution of a decree.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: fraudulently accepts, receives or claims any property or any interest therein, knowing he has no right or rightful claim
intending to prevent that property or interest from being taken as forfeiture, in satisfaction of a fine, or in execution of a decree or order
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 244, Page 67

description: 245.  Whoever fraudulently causes or suffers a decree or order to be passed against him at the suit of any person for a sum not due or for a larger sum than is due to such person or for any property or interest in property to which such person is not entitled, or fraudulently causes or suffers a decree or order to be executed against him after it has been satisfied, or for anything in respect of which it has been satisfied, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. Illustration. A institutes a suit against Z. Z, knowing that A is likely to obtain a decree against him, fraudulently suffers a judgment to pass against him for a larger amount at the suit of B, who has no just claim against him, in order that B, either on his own account or for the benefit of Z, may share in the proceeds of any sale of Z’s property which may be made under A’s decree. Z has committed an offence under this section.
summary: Criminalises fraudulent causing or suffering of a decree or order for an undue sum or property, or execution of such decree after satisfaction, including cases where a judgment is fraudulently suffered to benefit a third party.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: fraudulently causes or suffers a decree or order to be passed against him for a sum not due, a larger sum than due, or for property to which the claimant is not entitled
fraudulently causes or suffers a decree or order to be executed against him after it has been satisfied
explanation: A institutes a suit against Z. Z, knowing that A is likely to obtain a decree against him, fraudulently suffers a judgment to pass against him for a larger amount at the suit of B, who has no just claim against him, in order that B, either on his own account or for the benefit of Z, may share in the proceeds of any sale of Z’s property which may be made under A’s decree. Z has committed an offence under this section.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 245, Page 67

description: 246.  Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly, or with intent to injure or annoy any person, makes in a Court any claim which he knows to be false, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, and shall also be liable to fine.
summary: Penalises making a false claim in court with knowledge of its falsity, whether done fraudulently, dishonestly, or with intent to injure or annoy.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, and shall also be liable to fine
ingredients: makes in a Court any claim which he knows to be false
acts fraudulently or dishonestly, or with intent to injure or annoy
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 246, Page 68

description: 247.  Whoever fraudulently obtains a decree or order against any person for a sum not due, or for a larger sum than is due or for any property or interest in property to which he is not entitled, or fraudulently causes a decree or order to be executed against any person after it has been satisfied or for anything in respect of which it has been satisfied, or fraudulently suffers or permits any such act to be done in his name, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
summary: Criminalises fraudulent obtaining or execution of a decree or order for an undue sum or property, including execution after satisfaction, or permitting such acts in one's name.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: fraudulently obtains a decree or order against any person for a sum not due, a larger sum than due, or for property to which he is not entitled
fraudulently causes a decree or order to be executed after it has been satisfied
fraudulently suffers or permits any such act to be done in his name
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 247, Page 68

description: 248.  Whoever, with intent to cause injury to any person, institutes or causes to be instituted any criminal proceeding against that person, or falsely charges any person with having committed an offence, knowing that there is no just or lawful ground for such proceeding or charge against that person,— ( a ) shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine which may extend to two lakh rupees, or with both; ( b ) if such criminal proceeding be instituted on a false charge of an offence punishable with death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for ten years or upwards, shall be punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
summary: Penalises instituting criminal proceedings or false charges against a person with intent to cause injury, with higher punishment if the false charge relates to a serious offence such as death, life imprisonment or ten years or more.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine which may extend to two lakh rupees, or with both; if such criminal proceeding be instituted on a false charge of an offence punishable with death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for ten years or upwards, shall be punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine
ingredients: with intent to cause injury to any person, institutes or causes to be instituted any criminal proceeding against that person
falsely charges any person with having committed an offence, knowing that there is no just or lawful ground for such proceeding or charge
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 248, Page 68

description: 249.  Whenever an offence has been committed, whoever harbours or conceals a person whom he knows or has reason to believe to be the offender, with the intention of screening him from legal punishment shall,— ( a ) if the offence is punishable with death, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine; ( b )   if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; ( c ) if the offence is punishable with imprisonment which may extend to one year, and not to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence for
summary: Criminalises harbouring or concealing an offender with knowledge of his identity and intent to shield him from legal punishment, prescribing varying punishments based on the severity of the original offence.
punishment: if the offence is punishable with death, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine; if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; if the offence is punishable with imprisonment which may extend to one year, and not to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence for ... (text incomplete)
ingredients: harbours or conceals a person whom he knows or has reason to believe to be the offender
intention of screening him from legal punishment
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 249, Page 68

description: 250.  Whoever accepts or attempts to obtain, or agrees to accept, any gratification for himself or any other person, or any restitution of property to himself or any other person, in consideration of his concealing an offence or of his screening any person from legal punishment for any offence, or of his not proceeding against any person for the purpose of bringing him to legal punishment shall,–– ( a ) if the offence is punishable with death, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; ( b )   if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; ( c ) if the offence is punishable with imprisonment not extending to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term of imprisonment provided for the offence, or with fine, or with both.
summary: Section 250 penalises any person who accepts, attempts to obtain, or agrees to accept any gratification in exchange for concealing an offence, screening a person from legal punishment, or not proceeding against a person. The punishment varies according to the severity of the concealed offence, ranging up to seven years imprisonment and fine.
punishment: ( a ) if the offence is punishable with death, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; ( b )   if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; ( c ) if the offence is punishable with imprisonment not extending to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term of imprisonment provided for the offence, or with fine, or with both.
ingredients: accepts, attempts to obtain, or agrees to accept any gratification for self or another
gratification may be restitution of property
consideration is concealing an offence, screening a person from legal punishment, or not proceeding against a person
the concealed offence is punishable with death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment not exceeding ten years
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 250, Page 69

description: 251.  Whoever gives or causes, or offers or agrees to give or cause, any gratification to any person, or restores or causes the restoration of any property to any person, in consideration of that person’s concealing an offence, or of his screening any person from legal punishment for any offence, or of his not proceeding against any person for the purpose of bringing him to legal punishment shall,–– ( a ) if the offence is punishable with death, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; ( b ) if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life or with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; ( c ) if the offence is punishable with imprisonment not extending to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term of imprisonment provided for the offence, or with fine, or with both. Exception.— The provisions of this section and section 250 do not extend to any case in which the offence may lawfully be compounded.
summary: Section 251 criminalises offering, giving, or causing gratification to another person for that person’s act of concealing an offence, screening a person from legal punishment, or refraining from proceeding against a person. The penalty mirrors that of Section 250 and does not apply where the offence can be lawfully compounded.
punishment: ( a ) if the offence is punishable with death, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; ( b ) if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life or with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; ( c ) if the offence is punishable with imprisonment not extending to ten years, be punished with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term of imprisonment provided for the offence, or with fine, or with both.
ingredients: gives, causes, offers, or agrees to give gratification to any person
gratification may include restoration of property
consideration is the other person’s concealing an offence, screening a person from legal punishment, or not proceeding against a person
the concealed offence is punishable with death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment not exceeding ten years
exceptions: The provisions of this section and section 250 do not extend to any case in which the offence may lawfully be compounded.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 251, Page 69

description: 252.  Whoever takes or agrees or consents to take any gratification under pretence or on account of helping any person to recover any movable property of which he shall have been deprived by any offence punishable under this Sanhita ,  shall, unless he uses all means in his power to cause the offender to be apprehended and convicted of the Taking gift, etc., to screen an offender from punishment. Offering gift or restoration of property in consideration of screening offender. Taking gift to help to recover stolen property, etc.
summary: Section 252 deals with a person who takes or consents to take gratification offered under pretence of helping recover stolen movable property, but fails to use all means to cause the original offender’s apprehension, thereby screening the offender from punishment.
ingredients: takes, agrees, or consents to take any gratification
gratification is offered under pretence or for helping recover movable property deprived by an offence
does not use all means to cause the offender to be apprehended and convicted
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 252, Page 69

description: 253.  Whenever any person convicted of or charged with an offence, being in lawful custody for that offence, escapes from such custody, or whenever a public servant, in the exercise of the lawful powers of such public servant, orders a certain person to be apprehended for an offence, whoever, knowing of such escape or order for apprehension, harbours or conceals that person with the intention of preventing him from being apprehended, shall be punished in the manner following, namely:–– ( a ) if the offence for which the person was in custody or is ordered to be apprehended is punishable with death, he shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; ( b ) if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment for ten years, he shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, with or without fine; ( c ) if the offence is punishable with imprisonment which may extend to one year and not to ten years, he shall be punished with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term of the imprisonment provided for such offence, or with fine, or with both. Explanation.–– “Offence” in this section includes also any act or omission of which a person is alleged to have been guilty out of India, which, if he had been guilty of it in India, would have been punishable as an offence, and for which he is, under any law relating to extradition, or otherwise, liable to be apprehended or detained in custody in India, and every such act or omission shall, for the purposes of this section, be deemed to be punishable as if the accused person had been guilty of it in India. Exception .—The provisions of this section do not extend to the case in which the harbour or concealment is by the spouse of the person to be apprehended.
summary: Section 253 penalises anyone who, knowing that a person has escaped custody or is ordered to be apprehended, harbours or conceals that person to prevent apprehension. The punishment depends on the severity of the original offence, ranging up to seven years imprisonment and fine.
punishment: ( a ) if the offence for which the person was in custody or is ordered to be apprehended is punishable with death, he shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; ( b ) if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment for ten years, he shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, with or without fine; ( c ) if the offence is punishable with imprisonment which may extend to one year and not to ten years, he shall be punished with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term of the imprisonment provided for such offence, or with fine, or with both.
ingredients: knowing of an escape from lawful custody or an order for apprehension
harbours or conceals the escaped or ordered person
intention to prevent the person from being apprehended
the original offence is punishable with death, life imprisonment, ten years, or up to one year
exceptions: The provisions of this section do not extend to the case in which the harbour or concealment is by the spouse of the person to be apprehended.
explanation: “Offence” in this section includes also any act or omission of which a person is alleged to have been guilty out of India, which, if he had been guilty of it in India, would have been punishable as an offence, and for which he is, under any law relating to extradition, or otherwise, liable to be apprehended or detained in custody in India, and every such act or omission shall, for the purposes of this section, be deemed to be punishable as if the accused person had been guilty of it in India.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 253, Page 70

description: 254.  Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that any persons are about to commit or have recently committed robbery or dacoity, harbours them or any of them,
summary: Section 254 criminalises the act of harbouring persons who are about to commit or have recently committed robbery or dacoity, when the harbuer knows or has reason to believe of such intent or act.
ingredients: knowing or having reason to believe persons are about to commit or have recently committed robbery or dacoity
harbours those persons or any of them
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 254, Page 70

description: 255.  Whoever, being a public servant, knowingly disobeys any direction of the law as to the way in which he is to conduct himself as such public servant, intending thereby to save, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby save, any person from legal punishment, or subject him to a less punishment than that to which he is liable, or with intent to save, or knowing that he is likely thereby to save, any property from forfeiture or any charge to which it is liable by law, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
summary: A public servant who knowingly disobeys a legal direction with the intention or knowledge that it will save a person from punishment or protect property from forfeiture is liable to imprisonment up to two years, a fine, or both.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: public servant
knowing disobedience of a direction of law
intent or knowledge that the act will save a person from legal punishment or protect property from forfeiture
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 255, Page 70

description: 256 . Whoever, being a public servant, and being as such public servant, charged with the preparation of any record or other writing, frames that record or writing in a manner which he knows to be incorrect, with intent to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, loss or injury to the public or to any person, or with intent thereby to save, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby save, any person from legal punishment, or with intent to save, or knowing that he is likely thereby to save, any property from forfeiture or other charge to which it is liable by law, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
summary: A public servant who knowingly prepares an incorrect record or writing with intent to cause loss or to save a person from punishment or protect property from forfeiture faces imprisonment up to three years, a fine, or both.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: public servant
preparation of a record or writing
knowledge that the record is incorrect
intent to cause loss or injury, or intent/knowledge to save a person from punishment or protect property from forfeiture
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 256

description: 257.  Whoever, being a public servant, corruptly or maliciously makes or pronounces in any stage of a judicial proceeding, any report, order, verdict, or decision which he knows to be contrary to law, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
summary: A public servant who knowingly makes or pronounces a false report, order, verdict, or decision at any stage of a judicial proceeding is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years, a fine, or both.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: public servant
corrupt or malicious making or pronouncing
report, order, verdict or decision
knowledge that it is contrary to law
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 257, Page 71

description: 258.  Whoever, being in any office which gives him legal authority to commit persons for trial or to confinement, or to keep persons in confinement, corruptly or maliciously commits any person for trial or to confinement, or keeps any person in confinement, in the exercise of that authority knowing that in so doing he is acting contrary to law, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
summary: A person holding authority to commit or keep individuals in confinement who corruptly or maliciously exercises that authority contrary to law faces imprisonment up to seven years, a fine, or both.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: holder of legal authority to commit or keep persons in confinement
corrupt or malicious exercise of that authority
knowledge that the act is contrary to law
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 258, Page 71

description: 259.  Whoever, being a public servant, legally bound as such public servant to apprehend or to keep in confinement any person charged with or liable to be apprehended for an offence, intentionally omits to apprehend such person, or intentionally suffers such person to escape, or intentionally aids such person in escaping or attempting to escape from such confinement, shall be punished,–– ( a ) with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, with or without fine, if the person in confinement, or who ought to have been apprehended, was charged with, or liable to be apprehended for, an offence punishable with death; or ( b ) with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, with or without fine, if the person in confinement, or who ought to have been apprehended, was charged with, or liable to be apprehended for, an offence punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years; or ( c ) with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, with or without fine, if the person in confinement, or who ought to have been apprehended, was charged with, or liable to be apprehended for, an offence punishable with imprisonment for a term less than ten years.
summary: A public servant bound to apprehend or keep a person in confinement who intentionally fails to apprehend, allows escape, or aids escape is punished according to the seriousness of the offence for which the confined person was charged, ranging from up to seven years to two years imprisonment.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, with or without fine; imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, with or without fine; imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, with or without fine (as per clauses a, b, c)
ingredients: public servant
legal duty to apprehend or keep in confinement
intentional omission to apprehend, or intentional allowing/assisting escape
knowledge of the person's charge and its punishment tier
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 259, Page 71

description: 260 . Whoever, being a public servant, legally bound as such public servant to apprehend or to keep in confinement any person under sentence of a Court for any offence or lawfully committed to custody, intentionally omits to apprehend such person, or intentionally suffers such person to escape or intentionally aids such person in escaping or attempting to escape from such confinement, shall be punished,— ( a ) with imprisonment for life or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to fourteen years, with or without fine, if the person in confinement, or who ought to have been apprehended, is under sentence of death; ( b ) with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, with or without fine, if the person in confinement or who ought to have been apprehended, is subject, by a sentence of a Court, or by virtue of a commutation of such sentence, to imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term of ten years, or upwards; or ( c ) with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both, if the person in confinement or who ought to have been apprehended, is subject by a sentence of a Court to imprisonment for a term not extending to ten years or if the person was lawfully committed to custody.
summary: A public servant bound to apprehend or keep a sentenced person in custody who intentionally fails to apprehend, allows escape, or aids escape faces imprisonment ranging from life to three years, depending on the sentenced person's original punishment.
punishment: imprisonment for life or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to fourteen years, with or without fine; imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, with or without fine; imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both (as per clauses a, b, c)
ingredients: public servant
legal duty to apprehend or keep in confinement a person under court sentence
intentional omission to apprehend, or intentional allowing/assisting escape
knowledge of the sentenced person's punishment category
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 260

description: 261.  Whoever, being a public servant legally bound as such public servant to keep in confinement any person charged with or convicted of any offence or lawfully committed to custody, negligently suffers such person to escape from confinement, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
summary: A public servant who, through negligence, allows a confined person charged with or convicted of an offence to escape is liable to simple imprisonment up to two years, a fine, or both.
punishment: simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: public servant
legal duty to keep a person in confinement
negligence resulting in the person's escape
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 261, Page 72

description: 262.  Whoever intentionally offers any resistance or illegal obstruction to the lawful apprehension of himself for any offence with which he is charged or of which he has been convicted, or escapes or attempts to escape from any custody in which he is lawfully detained for any such offence, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. Explanation. —The punishment in this section is in addition to the punishment for which the person to be apprehended or detained in custody was liable for the offence with which he was charged, or of which he was convicted.
summary: Section 262 penalises a person who deliberately resists or obstructs his lawful arrest for an offence, or who escapes or tries to escape from lawful custody. The punishment is up to two years' imprisonment, a fine, or both, and is added to any sentence for the original offence.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: Intentionally offers any resistance or illegal obstruction to the lawful apprehension of himself for any offence with which he is charged or convicted
Escapes or attempts to escape from any custody in which he is lawfully detained for any such offence
explanation: The punishment in this section is in addition to the punishment for which the person to be apprehended or detained in custody was liable for the offence with which he was charged, or of which he was convicted.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 262, Page 72

description: 263.  Whoever, intentionally offers any resistance or illegal obstruction to the lawful apprehension of any other person for an offence, or rescues or attempts to rescue any other person from any custody in which that person is lawfully detained for an offence,— ( a ) shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both; or ( b ) if the person to be apprehended, or the person rescued or attempted to be rescued, is charged with or liable to be apprehended for an offence punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; or ( c ) if the person to be apprehended or rescued, or attempted to be rescued, is charged with or liable to be apprehended for an offence punishable with death, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; or ( d ) if the person to be apprehended or rescued, or attempted to be rescued, is liable under the sentence of a Court or by virtue of a commutation of such a sentence, to imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for a term of ten years or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; or ( e ) if the person to be apprehended or rescued, or attempted to be rescued, is under sentence of death, shall be punished with imprisonment for life or imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
summary: The section penalises anyone who intentionally resists or obstructs lawful apprehension of another person, or rescues them from custody. The punishment varies according to the seriousness of the offence for which the apprehended person is charged, ranging from up to two years to life imprisonment.
punishment: shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both; or ( b ) if the person to be apprehended, or the person rescued or attempted to be rescued, is charged with or liable to be apprehended for an offence punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; or ( c ) if the person to be apprehended or rescued, or attempted to be rescued, is charged with or liable to be apprehended for an offence punishable with death, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; or ( d ) if the person to be apprehended or rescued, or attempted to be rescued, is liable under the sentence of a Court or by virtue of a commutation of such a sentence, to imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for a term of ten years or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; or ( e ) if the person to be apprehended or rescued, or attempted to be rescued, is under sentence of death, shall be punished with imprisonment for life or imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding ten years, and shall also be liable to fine
ingredients: intentionally offers resistance or illegal obstruction to lawful apprehension of another person
or rescues/attempts to rescue another person from lawful custody
knowledge of the offence for which the apprehended person is charged determines the applicable punishment tier
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 263, Page 72

description: 264.  Whoever, being a public servant legally bound as such public servant to apprehend, or to keep in confinement, any person in any case not provided for in section 259, section 260 or section 261, or in any other law for the time being in force, omits to apprehend that person or suffers him to escape from confinement, shall be punished— ( a ) if he does so intentionally, with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both; and ( b ) if he does so negligently, with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
summary: A public servant who fails to apprehend or allows escape of a person in situations not covered by earlier sections is punished. Intentional omission attracts up to three years imprisonment, while negligent omission attracts up to two years simple imprisonment.
punishment: shall be punished— ( a ) if he does so intentionally, with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both; and ( b ) if he does so negligently, with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: public servant legally bound to apprehend or keep in confinement
omits to apprehend or allows escape of a person in a case not covered by sections 259-261 or other law
intention or negligence of the omission
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 264, Page 73

description: 265.  Whoever, in any case not provided for in section 262 or section 263 or in any other law for the time being in force, intentionally offers any resistance or illegal obstruction to the lawful apprehension of himself or of any other person, or escapes or attempts to escape from any custody in which he is lawfully detained, or rescues or attempts to rescue any other person from any custody in which that person is lawfully detained, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
summary: The section criminalises intentional resistance, obstruction, escape, or rescue in cases not covered by sections 262 or 263. The maximum punishment is six months imprisonment or a fine.
punishment: shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: intentionally offers resistance or illegal obstruction to lawful apprehension of self or another person
or escapes/attempts to escape from lawful custody
or rescues/attempts to rescue another person from lawful custody
the act occurs in a case not covered by sections 262 or 263 or any other law
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 265, Page 73

description: 266.  Whoever, having accepted any conditional remission of punishment, knowingly violates any condition on which such remission was granted, shall be punished with the punishment to which he was originally sentenced, if he has already suffered no part of that punishment, and if he has suffered any part of that punishment, then with so much of that punishment as he has not already suffered.
summary: If a person who received a conditional remission of punishment knowingly breaches any condition of that remission, they are liable to serve the original sentence, either in full or the remaining part not yet served.
punishment: shall be punished with the punishment to which he was originally sentenced, if he has already suffered no part of that punishment, and if he has suffered any part of that punishment, then with so much of that punishment as he has not already suffered
ingredients: accepted conditional remission of punishment
knowingly violates any condition of the remission
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 266, Page 73

description: 267.  Whoever, intentionally offers any insult, or causes any interruption to any public servant, while such public servant is sitting in any stage of a judicial proceeding, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: The section penalises intentional insult or interruption directed at a public servant who is participating in any stage of a judicial proceeding. Punishment is up to six months simple imprisonment, a fine up to Rs.5,000, or both.
punishment: shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both
ingredients: intentionally offers insult or causes interruption
to a public servant
while the public servant is sitting in any stage of a judicial proceeding
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 267, Page 73

description: 268.  Whoever, by personation or otherwise, shall intentionally cause, or knowingly suffer himself to be returned, empanelled or sworn as an assessor in any case in which he knows that he is not entitled by law to be so returned, empanelled or sworn, or knowing himself to have been so returned, empanelled or sworn contrary to law, shall voluntarily serve as such assessor, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
summary: Any person who, by impersonation or other means, intentionally causes or knowingly allows himself to be appointed as an assessor in a case where he is not legally entitled, is punishable. The maximum penalty is two years imprisonment or a fine, or both.
punishment: shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: by personation or otherwise, intentionally causes or knowingly suffers himself to be returned, empanelled or sworn as an assessor
knows he is not entitled by law to such appointment
or knows he has been appointed contrary to law
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 268, Page 73

description: 269.  Whoever, having been charged with an offence and released on bail bond or on bond, fails without sufficient cause (the burden of proving which shall lie upon him), to appear in Court in accordance with the terms of the bail or bond, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both. Explanation.— The punishment under this section is— ( a ) in addition to the punishment to which the offender would be liable on a conviction for the offence with which he has been charged; and ( b ) without prejudice to the power of the Court to order forfeiture of the bond.
summary: A person released on bail who fails to appear in court without sufficient cause is liable for up to one year imprisonment or a fine, in addition to any punishment for the original offence, and the court may also forfeit the bail bond.
punishment: shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: charged with an offence and released on bail bond or bond
fails without sufficient cause to appear in Court as per bail/bond terms
burden of proving sufficient cause lies on the offender
explanation: The punishment under this section is— ( a ) in addition to the punishment to which the offender would be liable on a conviction for the offence with which he has been charged; and ( b ) without prejudice to the power of the Court to order forfeiture of the bond.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 269, Page 73

description: 270.  A person is guilty of a public nuisance who does any act or is guilty of an illegal omission which causes any common injury, danger or annoyance to the public or to the people in general who dwell or occupy property in the vicinity, or which must necessarily cause injury, obstruction, danger or annoyance to persons who may have occasion to use any public right but a common nuisance is not excused on the ground that it causes some convenience or advantage.
summary: Any act or omission that causes common injury, danger, or annoyance to the public or nearby residents, or inevitably harms persons using a public right, constitutes a public nuisance. No benefit or convenience can excuse the nuisance.
ingredients: does any act or illegal omission
causes common injury, danger or annoyance to the public or nearby occupants
or necessarily causes injury, obstruction, danger or annoyance to persons using a public right
the nuisance is not excused by any convenience or advantage
