sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 270, Page 73

description: Whoever unlawfully or negligently does any act which is, and which he knows or has reason to believe to be, likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
summary: A person who unlawfully or negligently performs an act that he knows or has reason to believe may spread a disease dangerous to life is liable for punishment.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
ingredients: unlawfully or negligently does any act
knows or has reason to believe the act is likely to spread infection of a disease dangerous to life
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 271, Page 74

description: Whoever malignantly does any act which is, and which he knows or has reason to believe to be, likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
summary: A person who maliciously performs an act that he knows or has reason to believe may spread a disease dangerous to life is punishable.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
ingredients: malignantly does any act
knows or has reason to believe the act is likely to spread infection of a disease dangerous to life
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 272, Page 74

description: Whoever knowingly disobeys any rule made by the Government for putting any mode of transport into a state of quarantine, or for regulating the intercourse of any such transport in a state of quarantine or for regulating the intercourse between places where an infectious disease prevails and other places, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
summary: Anyone who knowingly violates government quarantine rules for transport is liable for imprisonment up to six months or a fine.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
ingredients: knowingly disobeys any rule made by the Government for quarantine of transport
the rule relates to putting transport in quarantine or regulating its movement
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 273, Page 74

description: Whoever adulterates any article of food or drink, so as to make such article noxious as food or drink, intending to sell such article as food or drink, or knowing it to be likely that the same will be sold as food or drink, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Adulterating food or drink to make it harmful, with intent or knowledge that it will be sold as food or drink, attracts imprisonment up to six months or a fine up to Rs 5,000.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
ingredients: adulterates any article of food or drink making it noxious
intends to sell it as food or drink or knows it is likely to be sold as such
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 274, Page 74

description: Whoever sells, or offers or exposes for sale, as food or drink, any article which has been rendered or has become noxious, or is in a state unfit for food or drink, knowing or having reason to believe that the same is noxious as food or drink, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Selling or offering for sale food or drink that is noxious or unfit, with knowledge or reason to believe it is noxious, is punishable by imprisonment up to six months or a fine up to Rs 5,000.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
ingredients: sells, offers or exposes for sale a noxious or unfit article as food or drink
knowing or having reason to believe the article is noxious
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 275

description: Whoever adulterates any drug or medical preparation in such a manner as to lessen the efficacy or change the operation of such drug or medical preparation, or to make it noxious, intending that it shall be sold or used for, or knowing it to be likely that it will be sold or used for, any medicinal purpose, as if it had not undergone such adulteration, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Adulterating drugs or medical preparations to reduce efficacy, alter operation, or make them harmful, with intent or knowledge of sale/use as genuine, attracts imprisonment up to one year or a fine up to Rs 5,000.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
ingredients: adulterates any drug or medical preparation to lessen efficacy, change operation, or make it noxious
intends it to be sold or used medicinally as if unadulterated or knows it is likely to be sold/used as such
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 276, Page 74

description: Whoever, knowing any drug or medical preparation to have been adulterated in such a manner as to lessen its efficacy, to change its operation, or to render it noxious, sells the same, or offers or exposes it for sale, or issues it from any dispensary for medicinal purposes as unadulterated, or causes it to be used for medicinal purposes by any person not knowing of the adulteration, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees , or with both.
summary: Selling or dispensing a known adulterated drug as genuine, or causing its use by unaware persons, is punishable by imprisonment up to six months or a fine up to Rs 5,000.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees , or with both.
ingredients: knowing a drug or medical preparation is adulterated to lessen efficacy, change operation, or make it noxious
sells, offers, exposes for sale, or issues it as unadulterated
or causes its use by a person unaware of the adulteration
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 277, Page 74

description: Whoever knowingly sells, or offers or exposes for sale, or issues from a dispensary for medicinal purposes, any drug or medical preparation, as a different drug or medical preparation, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Knowingly selling or dispensing a drug as a different drug or preparation is punishable by imprisonment up to six months or a fine up to Rs 5,000.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
ingredients: knowingly sells, offers, exposes for sale, or issues any drug or medical preparation
as a different drug or medical preparation
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 278, Page 74

description: Whoever voluntarily corrupts or fouls the water of any public spring or reservoir, so as to render it less fit for the purpose for which it is ordinarily used, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Voluntarily fouling water of a public spring or reservoir, making it less fit for its ordinary use, attracts imprisonment up to six months or a fine up to Rs 5,000.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
ingredients: voluntarily corrupts or fouls water of any public spring or reservoir
renders the water less fit for its ordinary purpose
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 279, Page 74

description: Whoever voluntarily vitiates the atmosphere in any place so as to make it noxious to the health of persons in general dwelling or carrying on business in the neighbourhood or passing along a public way, shall be punished with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees.
summary: Voluntarily polluting the atmosphere to make it harmful to health is punishable by a fine up to Rs 1,000.
punishment: fine which may extend to one thousand rupees.
ingredients: voluntarily vitiates the atmosphere in any place
makes it noxious to the health of persons in the vicinity
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 280, Page 74

description: Whoever drives any vehicle, or rides, on any public way in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Rash or negligent driving or riding on a public way that endangers life or may cause injury is punishable by imprisonment up to six months or a fine up to Rs 1,000.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
ingredients: drives any vehicle or rides on any public way
in a rash or negligent manner
that endangers human life or is likely to cause hurt or injury to any person
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 281, Page 74

description: Whoever navigates any vessel in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to ten thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Rash or negligent navigation of a vessel that endangers life or may cause injury is punishable by imprisonment up to six months or a fine up to Rs 10,000.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to ten thousand rupees, or with both.
ingredients: navigates any vessel
in a rash or negligent manner
that endangers human life or is likely to cause hurt or injury to any person
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 282, Page 75

description: Whoever exhibits any false light, mark or buoy, intending or knowing it to be likely that such exhibition will mislead any navigator, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and with fine which shall not be less than ten thousand rupees.
summary: Displaying false lights, marks, or buoys with intent or knowledge that they may mislead navigators is punishable by imprisonment up to seven years and a fine of at least Rs 10,000.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and with fine which shall not be less than ten thousand rupees.
ingredients: exhibits any false light, mark or buoy
intending or knowing it is likely to mislead any navigator
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 283, Page 75

description: Whoever knowingly or negligently conveys, or causes to be conveyed for hire, any person by water in any vessel, when that vessel is in such a state or so loaded as to endanger the life of that person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Knowingly or negligently transporting a person by water in a vessel that is unsafe or overloaded, endangering life, is punishable by imprisonment up to six months or a fine up to Rs 5,000.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
ingredients: knowingly or negligently conveys or causes to be conveyed for hire any person by water in any vessel
the vessel is in a state or loaded so as to endanger the life of that person
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 284, Page 75

description: Whoever, by doing any act, or by omitting to take order with any property in his possession or under his charge, causes danger, obstruction or injury to any person in any public way or public line of navigation, shall be punished with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees.
summary: Causing danger, obstruction, or injury to any person on a public way or navigation line by act or omission concerning property in one's possession is punishable by a fine up to Rs 5,000.
punishment: fine which may extend to five thousand rupees.
ingredients: does any act or omits to take order with any property in his possession or charge
causes danger, obstruction or injury to any person in any public way or public line of navigation
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 285, Page 75

description: Whoever does, with any poisonous substance, any act in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any person or knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any poisonous substance in his possession as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life from such poisonous substance, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Rash or negligent handling of poisonous substances that endangers life or causes injury, or failure to take adequate safety measures, is punishable by imprisonment up to six months or a fine up to Rs 5,000.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
ingredients: does, with any poisonous substance, any act in a rash or negligent manner that endangers human life or may cause hurt or injury
or knowingly or negligently omits to take sufficient order with the poisonous substance to guard against probable danger
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 286, Page 75

description: Whoever does, with fire or any combustible matter, any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person or knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any fire or any combustible matter in his possession as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life from such fire or combustible matter, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to two thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Rash or negligent use of fire or combustible material that endangers life or may cause injury, or failure to take adequate safety measures, is punishable by imprisonment up to six months or a fine up to Rs 2,000.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to two thousand rupees, or with both.
ingredients: does, with fire or any combustible matter, any act rashly or negligently that endangers human life or may cause hurt or injury
or knowingly or negligently omits to take sufficient order with the fire or combustible matter to guard against probable danger
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 287, Page 75

description: ently as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person, or knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any explosive substance in his possession as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life from that substance, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Any person who rashly or negligently handles an explosive substance in a manner that endangers human life, or fails to take adequate precautions against probable danger, is liable for imprisonment up to six months or a fine up to five thousand rupees.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both
ingredients: rash or negligent act with explosive substance endangering human life
omission to take sufficient precaution against probable danger to human life from explosive substance
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 288, Page 75

description: Whoever does, with any machinery, any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person or knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any machinery in his possession or under his care as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life from such machinery, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Any person who rashly or negligently uses machinery in a way that endangers human life, or fails to take adequate safety measures, is punishable with up to six months imprisonment or a fine up to five thousand rupees.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both
ingredients: rash or negligent act with machinery endangering human life
omission to take sufficient precaution against probable danger to human life from machinery
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 289, Page 75

description: Whoever, in pulling down, repairing or constructing any building, knowingly or negligently omits to take such measures with that building as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life from the fall of that building, or of any part thereof, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Any person who, while pulling down, repairing or constructing a building, negligently fails to take adequate safety measures against the risk of collapse, is liable for up to six months imprisonment or a fine up to five thousand rupees.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both
ingredients: rash or negligent act in pulling down, repairing or constructing a building endangering human life
omission to take sufficient precaution against probable danger of building collapse
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 290, Page 75

description: Whoever knowingly or negligently omits to take such measures with any animal in his possession as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life, or any probable danger of grievous hurt from such animal, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Any person who, knowing or negligently, fails to take adequate precautions with an animal in his possession to prevent danger to human life or grievous hurt, is punishable with up to six months imprisonment or a fine up to five thousand rupees.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both
ingredients: omission to take sufficient precaution with animal in possession to guard against probable danger to human life or grievous hurt
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 291, Page 76

description: Whoever commits a public nuisance in any case not otherwise punishable by this Sanhita shall be punished with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees.
summary: Any person who creates a public nuisance that is not covered by any other provision of the Sanhita is liable to a fine up to one thousand rupees.
punishment: fine which may extend to one thousand rupees
ingredients: commits a public nuisance not otherwise punishable by this Sanhita
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 292, Page 76

description: Whoever repeats or continues a public nuisance, having been enjoined by any public servant who has lawful authority to issue such injunction not to repeat or continue such nuisance, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees ,  or with both.
summary: Any person who persists in a public nuisance after a lawful injunction is issued may be imprisoned up to six months or fined up to five thousand rupees.
punishment: simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both
ingredients: repeats or continues a public nuisance after lawful injunction
injunction issued by public servant with lawful authority
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 293, Page 76

description: ( 1 ) For the purposes of sub-section ( 2 ), a book, pamphlet, paper, writing, drawing, painting, representation, figure or any other object, including display of any content in electronic form shall be deemed to be obscene if it is lascivious or appeals to the prurient interest or if its effect, or (where it comprises two or more distinct items) the effect of any one of its items, is, if taken as a whole, such as to tend to deprave and corrupt persons who are likely, having regard to all relevant circumstances, to read, see or hear the matter contained or embodied in it. ( 2 ) Whoever— ( a ) sells, lets to hire, distributes, publicly exhibits or in any manner puts into circulation, or for purposes of sale, hire, distribution, public exhibition or circulation, makes, produces or has in his possession any obscene book, pamphlet, paper, drawing, painting, representation or figure or any other obscene object whatsoever in whatever manner; or ( b ) imports, exports or conveys any obscene object for any of the purposes aforesaid, or knowing or having reason to believe that such object will be sold, let to hire, distributed or publicly exhibited or in any manner put into circulation; or ( c ) takes part in or receives profits from any business in the course of which he knows or has reason to believe that any such obscene objects are, for any of the purposes aforesaid, made produced, purchased, kept, imported, exported, conveyed, publicly exhibited or in any manner put into circulation; or ( d ) advertises or makes known by any means whatsoever that any person is engaged or is ready to engage in any act which is an offence under this section, or that any such obscene object can be procured from or through any person; or ( e ) offers or attempts to do any act which is an offence under this section, shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, and with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, and, in the event of a second or subsequent conviction, with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and also with fine which may extend to ten thousand rupees. Exception. —This section does not extend to— ( a ) any book, pamphlet, paper, writing, drawing, painting, representation or figure— ( i ) the publication of which is proved to be justified as being for the public good on the ground that such book, pamphlet, paper, writing, drawing, painting, representation or figure is in the interest of science, literature, art or learning or other objects of general concern; or ( ii ) which is kept or used  bona fide  for religious purposes; ( b ) any representation sculptured, engraved, painted or otherwise represented on or in— ( i ) any ancient monument within the meaning of theAncient Monuments andArchaeological Sites and RemainsAct, 1958; or ( ii ) any temple, or on any car used for the conveyance of idols, or kept or used for any religious purpose.
summary: Section 294 defines obscenity and criminalises the sale, distribution, exhibition, import, export, advertisement, or any business dealing with obscene objects, with higher penalties on subsequent convictions. Certain publications for scientific, literary, artistic, or religious purposes, and representations on ancient monuments or temples are exempted.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, and with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, and, in the event of a second or subsequent conviction, with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and also with fine which may extend to ten thousand rupees
ingredients: selling, letting to hire, distributing, publicly exhibiting or putting into circulation obscene objects
importing, exporting or conveying obscene objects
participating in business knowing obscene objects are involved
advertising or making known engagement in obscene acts or availability of obscene objects
offering or attempting any act constituting offence under this section
exceptions: This section does not extend to— ( a ) any book, pamphlet, paper, writing, drawing, painting, representation or figure— ( i ) the publication of which is proved to be justified as being for the public good on the ground that such book, pamphlet, paper, writing, drawing, painting, representation or figure is in the interest of science, literature, art or learning or other objects of general concern; or ( ii ) which is kept or used  bona fide  for religious purposes; ( b ) any representation sculptured, engraved, painted or otherwise represented on or in— ( i ) any ancient monument within the meaning of theAncient Monuments andArchaeological Sites and RemainsAct, 1958; or ( ii ) any temple, or on any car used for the conveyance of idols, or kept or used for any religious purpose.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 294, Page 76

description: Whoever sells, lets to hire, distributes, exhibits or circulates to any child any such obscene object as is referred to in section 294, or offers or attempts so to do, shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and with fine which may extend to two thousand rupees, and, in the event of a second or subsequent conviction, with imprisonment of either descri
summary: Any person who sells, hires, distributes, exhibits or circulates obscene objects to a child, or attempts to do so, faces imprisonment up to three years and a fine up to two thousand rupees on first conviction, with harsher penalties on subsequent convictions.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and with fine which may extend to two thousand rupees
ingredients: selling, letting to hire, distributing, exhibiting or circulating obscene objects to any child
offering or attempting to do any of the above
keywords: sale of obscene material to minors
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 295

description: Whoever, to the annoyance of others,— ( a ) does any obscene act in any public place; or ( b ) sings, recites or utters any obscene song, ballad or words, in or near any public place, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
summary: Any person who commits an obscene act or sings/recites obscene material in a public place, causing annoyance, is liable for imprisonment up to three months, a fine up to Rs 1,000, or both.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both
ingredients: does any obscene act in any public place
sings, recites or utters any obscene song, ballad or words in or near any public place
to the annoyance of others
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 296

description: ( 1 ) Whoever keeps any office or place for the purpose of drawing any lottery not being a State lottery or a lottery authorised by the State Government, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both. ( 2 ) Whoever publishes any proposal to pay any sum, or to deliver any goods, or to do or forbear from doing anything for the benefit of any person, on any event or contingency relative or applicable to the drawing of any ticket, lot, number or figure in any such lottery, shall be punished with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees.
summary: Keeping an office for conducting an unauthorised lottery is punishable with imprisonment up to six months or a fine, and publishing any proposal related to such a lottery attracts a fine up to Rs 5,000.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both; fine which may extend to five thousand rupees
ingredients: keeps any office or place for the purpose of drawing any lottery not being a State lottery or a lottery authorised by the State Government

publishes any proposal to pay any sum, deliver any goods, or do/forbear from doing anything for the benefit of any person on any event or contingency related to the drawing of any ticket, lot, number or figure in such lottery
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 297, Page 77

description: Whoever destroys, damages or defiles any place of worship, or any object held sacred by any class of persons with the intention of thereby insulting the religion of any class of persons or with the knowledge that any class of persons is likely to consider such destruction, damage or defilement as an insult to their religion, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
summary: Destruction, damage or defilement of a place of worship or sacred object, intended to insult a religion or knowing it will be considered insulting, is punishable with imprisonment up to two years, a fine, or both.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: destroys, damages or defiles any place of worship or any object held sacred
with the intention of insulting the religion of any class of persons
or with knowledge that any class of persons is likely to consider such act as an insult to their religion
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 298, Page 77

description: Whoever, with deliberate and malicious intention of outraging the religious feelings of any class of citizens of India, by words, either spoken or written, or by signs or by visible representations or through electronic means or otherwise, insults or attempts to insult the religion or the religious beliefs of that class, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
summary: Deliberately and maliciously outraging the religious feelings of any class of citizens through words, signs, representations or electronic means, with intent to insult religion or beliefs, is punishable with imprisonment up to three years, a fine, or both.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: deliberate and malicious intention of outraging the religious feelings of any class of citizens
insults or attempts to insult the religion or religious beliefs of that class by words, signs, visible representations, electronic means or otherwise
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 299, Page 77

description: Whoever voluntarily causes disturbance to any assembly lawfully engaged in the performance of religious worship, or religious ceremonies, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
summary: Voluntarily disturbing a lawfully assembled group performing religious worship or ceremonies is punishable with imprisonment up to one year, a fine, or both.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: voluntarily causes disturbance to any assembly lawfully engaged in the performance of religious worship or religious ceremonies
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 300, Page 77

description: Whoever, with the intention of wounding the feelings of any person, or of insulting the religion of any person, or with the knowledge that the feelings of any person are likely to be wounded, or that the religion of any person is likely to be insulted thereby, commits any trespass in any place of worship or on any place of sepulchre, or any place set apart for the performance of funeral rites or as a depository for the remains of the dead, or offers any indignity to any human corpse, or causes disturbance to any persons assembled for the performance of funeral ceremonies, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
summary: Intentionally wounding a person's feelings or insulting their religion, or knowing it is likely, by trespassing in places of worship, sepulchres, funeral rites locations, offering indignity to a corpse, or disturbing funeral assemblies, attracts imprisonment up to one year, a fine, or both.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: intention of wounding the feelings of any person or insulting the religion of any person
knowledge that such feelings are likely to be wounded or religion likely to be insulted
commits any trespass in any place of worship, sepulchre, place for funeral rites, or depository of remains
offers any indignity to any human corpse
causes disturbance to persons assembled for funeral ceremonies
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 301, Page 78

description: Whoever, with the deliberate intention of wounding the religious feelings of any person, utters any word or makes any sound in the hearing of that person or makes any gesture in the sight of that person or places any object in the sight of that person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
summary: Deliberately wounding a person's religious feelings by uttering words, making sounds, gestures, or placing objects within their perception is punishable with imprisonment up to one year, a fine, or both.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: deliberate intention of wounding the religious feelings of any person
utters any word or makes any sound in the hearing of that person
makes any gesture in the sight of that person
places any object in the sight of that person
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 302, Page 78

title: Of theft
description: ( 1 ) Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any movable property out of the possession of any person without that person’s consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft. Explanation 1.—A thing so long as it is attached to the earth, not being movable property, is not the subject of theft; but it becomes capable of being the subject of theft as soon as it is severed from the earth. Explanation 2.—A moving effected by the same act which affects the severance may be a theft. Explanation 3.—A person is said to cause a thing to move by removing an obstacle which prevented it from moving or by separating it from any other thing, as well as by actually moving it. Explanation 4.—A person, who by any means causes an animal to move, is said to move that animal, and to move everything which, in consequence of the motion so caused, is moved by that animal. Explanation 5.—The consent mentioned in this section may be express or implied, and may be given either by the person in possession, or by any person having for that purpose authority either express or implied. Illustrations. ( a )A cuts down a tree on Z’s ground, with the intention of dishonestly taking the tree out of Z’s possession without Z’s consent. Here, as soon as A has severed the tree in order to such taking, he has committed theft. ( b ) A puts a bait for dogs in his pocket, and thus induces Z’s dog to follow it. Here, if A’s intention be dishonestly to take the dog out of Z’s possession without Z’s consent. A has committed theft as soon as Z’s dog has begun to follow A. ( c ) A meets a bullock carrying a box of treasure. He drives the bullock in a certain direction, in order that he may dishonestly take the treasure. As soon as the bullock begins to move, A has committed theft of the treasure. ( d ) A being Z’s servant, and entrusted by Z with the care of Z’s plate, dishonestly runs away with the plate, without Z’s consent. A has committed theft. ( e ) Z, going on a journey, entrusts his plate toA, the keeper of a warehouse, till Z shall return.Acarries the plate to a goldsmith and sells it. Here the plate was not in Z’s possession. It could not therefore be taken out of Z’s possession, and A has not committed theft, though he may have committed criminal breach of trust. ( f )A finds a ring belonging to Z on a table in the house which Z occupies. Here the ring is in Z’s possession, and if A dishonestly removes it, A commits theft. ( g ) A finds a ring lying on the hig
summary: Theft is defined as dishonestly moving any movable property with the intention of taking it out of another's possession without consent. Explanations clarify what constitutes movable property, consent, and how movement is caused; illustrations provide examples.
ingredients: intending to take dishonestly any movable property out of the possession of any person without that person’s consent
moves that property in order to such taking
explanation: Explanation 1.—A thing so long as it is attached to the earth, not being movable property, is not the subject of theft; but it becomes capable of being the subject of theft as soon as it is severed from the earth. Explanation 2.—A moving effected by the same act which affects the severance may be a theft. Explanation 3.—A person is said to cause a thing to move by removing an obstacle which prevented it from moving or by separating it from any other thing, as well as by actually moving it. Explanation 4.—A person, who by any means causes an animal to move, is said to move that animal, and to move everything which, in consequence of the motion so caused, is moved by that animal. Explanation 5.—The consent mentioned in this section may be express or implied, and may be given either by the person in possession, or by any person having for that purpose authority either express or implied. Here, as soon as A has severed the tree in order to such taking, he has committed theft. ( b ) A puts a bait for dogs in his pocket, and thus induces Z’s dog to follow it. Here, if A’s intention be dishonestly to take the dog out of Z’s possession without Z’s consent. A has committed theft as soon as Z’s dog has begun to follow A. ( c ) A meets a bullock carrying a box of treasure. He drives the bullock in a certain direction, in order that he may dishonestly take the treasure. As soon as the bullock begins to move, A has committed theft of the treasure. ( d ) A being Z’s servant, and entrusted by Z with the care of Z’s plate, dishonestly runs away with the plate, without Z’s consent. A has committed theft. ( e ) Z, going on a journey, entrusts his plate toA, the keeper of a warehouse, till Z shall return.Acarries the plate to a goldsmith and sells it. Here the plate was not in Z’s possession. It could not therefore be taken out of Z’s possession, and A has not committed theft, though he may have committed criminal breach of trust. ( f )A finds a ring belonging to Z on a table in the house which Z occupies. Here the ring is in Z’s possession, and if A dishonestly removes it, A commits theft. ( g ) A finds a ring lying on the hig
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 303, Page 78

description: (1) Theft is snatching if, in order to commit theft, the offender suddenly or quickly or forcibly seizes or secures or grabs or takes away from any person or from his possession any movable property. (2) Whoever commits snatching, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
summary: Section 304 defines "snatching" as a rapid or forceful seizure of movable property and prescribes imprisonment up to three years and a fine for the offence.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
ingredients: sudden or quick or forcible seizure
movable property
taken from any person or his possession
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 304, Page 80

description: Whoever commits theft— (a) in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling or used for the custody of property; or (b) of any means of transport used for the transport of goods or passengers; or (c) of any article or goods from any means of transport used for the transport of goods or passengers; or (d) of idol or icon in any place of worship; or (e) of any property of the Government or of a local authority, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
summary: Section 305 prescribes a higher penalty for theft committed in dwellings, vehicles, places of worship, or government/property of local authorities, with imprisonment up to seven years and a fine.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
ingredients: theft
in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling or for custody of property
or in any means of transport used for transport of goods or passengers
or of any article or goods from any means of transport used for transport of goods or passengers
or of idol or icon in any place of worship
or of any property of the Government or of a local authority
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 305

description: Whoever, being a clerk or servant, or being employed in the capacity of a clerk or servant, commits theft in respect of any property in the possession of his master or employer, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
summary: Section 306 deals with theft by a clerk or servant of property belonging to their master or employer, punishable by up to seven years imprisonment and a fine.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
ingredients: theft
by a clerk or servant
property in possession of master or employer
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 306, Page 80

description: Whoever commits theft, having made preparation for causing death, or hurt, or restraint, or fear of death, or of hurt, or of restraint, to any person, in order to the committing of such theft, or in order to the effecting of his escape after the committing of such theft, or in order to the retaining of property taken by such theft, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine. Illustrations. (a)Acommits theft on property in Z’s possession; and while committing this theft, he has a loaded pistol under his garment, having provided this pistol for the purpose of hurting Z in case Z should resist. A has committed the offence defined in this section. (b)Apicks Z’s pocket, having posted several of his companions near him, in order that they may restrain Z, if Z should perceive what is passing and should resist, or should attempt to apprehend A. A has committed the offence defined in this section.
summary: Section 307 imposes a stringent penalty for theft where the offender prepares or uses threats of death, hurt, or restraint to facilitate the theft, escape, or retention of stolen property, with imprisonment up to ten years and a fine.
punishment: rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
ingredients: theft
preparation for causing death, hurt, restraint, or fear thereof
purpose of committing theft, escaping after theft, or retaining stolen property
explanation: Illustrations. (a)Acommits theft on property in Z’s possession; and while committing this theft, he has a loaded pistol under his garment, having provided this pistol for the purpose of hurting Z in case Z should resist. A has committed the offence defined in this section. (b)Apicks Z’s pocket, having posted several of his companions near him, in order that they may restrain Z, if Z should perceive what is passing and should resist, or should attempt to apprehend A. A has committed the offence defined in this section.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 307, Page 80

description: (1) Whoever intentionally puts any person in fear of any injury to that person, or to any other, and thereby dishonestly induces the person so put in fear to deliver to any person any property, or valuable security or anything signed or sealed which may be converted into a valuable security, commits extortion. Illustrations. (a)A threatens to publish a defamatory libel concerning Z unless Z gives him money. He thus induces Z to give him money. A has committed extortion. (b)Athreatens Z that he will keep Z’s child in wrongful confinement, unless Z will sign and deliver toAa promissory note binding Z to pay certain monies toA. Z signs and delivers the note.A has committed extortion. (c) A threatens to send club-men to plough up Z’s field unless Z will sign and deliver to B a bond binding Z under a penalty to deliver certain produce to B, and thereby induces Z to sign and deliver the bond. A has committed extortion.
summary: Section 308 defines extortion as the intentional creation of fear of injury to induce a person to deliver property or securities, with illustrative examples of how the offence may be committed.
ingredients: intentionally puts any person in fear of injury
dishonestly induces the person to deliver any property, valuable security, or anything signed or sealed which may be converted into a valuable security
explanation: Illustrations. (a)A threatens to publish a defamatory libel concerning Z unless Z gives him money. He thus induces Z to give him money. A has committed extortion. (b)Athreatens Z that he will keep Z’s child in wrongful confinement, unless Z will sign and deliver toAa promissory note binding Z to pay certain monies toA. Z signs and delivers the note.A has committed extortion. (c) A threatens to send club-men to plough up Z’s field unless Z will sign and deliver to B a bond binding Z under a penalty to deliver certain produce to B, and thereby induces Z to sign and deliver the bond. A has committed extortion.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 308, Page 80

description: ( 1 ) In all robbery there is either theft or extortion. ( 2 ) Theft is robbery if, in order to the committing of the theft, or in committing the theft, or in carrying away or attempting to carry away property obtained by the theft, the offender, for that end voluntarily causes or attempts to cause to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint, or fear of instant death or of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint. ( 3 ) Extortion is robbery if the offender, at the time of committing the extortion, is in the presence of the person put in fear, and commits the extortion by putting that person in fear of instant death, of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint to that person or to some other person, and, by so putting in fear, induces the person so put in fear then and there to deliver up the thing extorted. Explanation .—The offender is said to be present if he is sufficiently near to put the other person in fear of instant death, of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint. Illustrations. ( a ) A holds Z down, and fraudulently takes Z’s money and jewels from Z’s clothes, without Z’s consent. Here A has committed theft, and, in order to the committing of that theft, has voluntarily caused wrongful restraint to Z. A has therefore committed robbery. ( b ) A meets Z on the high road, shows a pistol, and demands Z’s purse. Z, in consequence, surrenders his purse. Here A has extorted the purse from Z by putting him in fear of instant hurt, and being at the time of committing the extortion in his presence. A has therefore committed robbery. ( c ) A meets Z and Z’s child on the high road. A takes the child, and threatens to fling it down a precipice, unless Z delivers his purse. Z, in consequence, delivers his purse. Here A has extorted the purse from Z, by causing Z to be in fear of instant hurt to the child who is there present. A has therefore committed robbery on Z. ( d ) A obtains property from Z by saying—“Your child is in the hands of my gang, and will be put to death unless you send us ten thousand rupees”. This is extortion, and punishable as such; but it is not robbery, unless Z is put in fear of the instant death of his child. ( 4 ) Whoever commits robbery shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; and, if the robbery be committed on the highway between sunset and sunrise, the imprisonment may be extended to fourteen years. ( 5 ) Whoever attempts to commit robbery shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. ( 6 ) If any person, in committing or in attempting to commit robbery, voluntarily causes hurt, such person, and any other person jointly concerned in committing or attempting to commit such robbery, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
summary: Section 309 defines robbery as theft or extortion accompanied by causing or attempting to cause instant death, hurt or wrongful restraint, and sets out punishments ranging from up to ten years rigorous imprisonment to life imprisonment if hurt is caused. It also provides explanations and illustrations of the concept.
punishment: Whoever commits robbery shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; and, if the robbery be committed on the highway between sunset and sunrise, the imprisonment may be extended to fourteen years.
ingredients: theft or extortion
voluntarily causes or attempts to cause death, hurt or wrongful restraint, or fear of instant death, instant hurt or instant wrongful restraint
presence of the victim (for extortion) while putting them in fear
inducement of victim to deliver the thing extorted
explanation: The offender is said to be present if he is sufficiently near to put the other person in fear of instant death, of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 309, Page 81

description: ( 1 ) When five or more persons conjointly commit or attempt to commit a robbery, or where the whole number of persons conjointly committing or attempting to commit a robbery, and persons present and aiding such commission or attempt, amount to five or more, every person so committing, attempting or aiding, is said to commit dacoity. ( 2 ) Whoever commits dacoity shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine. ( 3 ) If any one of five or more persons, who are conjointly committing dacoity, commits murder in so committing dacoity, every one of those persons shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than ten years, and shall also be liable to fine. ( 4 ) Whoever makes any preparation for committing dacoity, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine. ( 5 ) Whoever is one of five or more persons assembled for the purpose of committing dacoity, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. ( 6 ) Whoever belongs to a gang of persons associated for the purpose of habitually committing dacoity, shall be punished with
summary: Section 310 defines dacoity as the commission or attempt of robbery by five or more persons, and prescribes severe punishments including life imprisonment, death, or rigorous imprisonment up to ten years, along with fines, for the act, its preparation, and related conspiracies.
punishment: Whoever commits dacoity shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
ingredients: five or more persons conjointly committing or attempting robbery
or five or more persons present and aiding the commission or attempt of robbery
joint participation or assistance in the act
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 310, Page 82

title: Dacoity. Robbery, or dacoity, with attempt to cause death or grievous hurt. Attempt to commit robbery or dacoity when armed with deadly weapon.
description: If, at the time of committing robbery or dacoity, the offender uses any deadly weapon, or causes grievous hurt to any person, or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt to any person, the imprisonment with which such offender shall be punished shall not be less than seven years.
summary: When a person commits robbery or dacoity using a deadly weapon, causing or attempting grievous hurt or death, the minimum imprisonment is seven years.
punishment: the imprisonment with which such offender shall be punished shall not be less than seven years
ingredients: commission of robbery or dacoity
use of any deadly weapon
causing grievous hurt or attempting to cause death or grievous hurt
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 311, Page 82

title: Dacoity. Robbery, or dacoity, with attempt to cause death or grievous hurt. Attempt to commit robbery or dacoity when armed with deadly weapon.
description: If, at the time of attempting to commit robbery or dacoity, the offender is armed with any deadly weapon, the imprisonment with which such offender shall be punished shall not be less than seven years.
summary: Attempting robbery or dacoity while armed with a deadly weapon attracts a minimum imprisonment of seven years.
punishment: the imprisonment with which such offender shall be punished shall not be less than seven years
ingredients: attempt to commit robbery or dacoity
being armed with any deadly weapon
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 312, Page 82

title: Punishment for belonging to gang of robbers, etc.
description: Whoever belongs to any gang of persons associated in habitually committing theft or robbery, and not being a gang of dacoits, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
summary: Membership in a gang habitually committing theft or robbery (excluding dacoit gangs) is punishable with up to seven years of rigorous imprisonment and a fine.
punishment: punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine
ingredients: belonging to a gang
gang habitually commits theft or robbery
gang is not a gang of dacoits
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 313, Page 83

title: Dishonest misappropriation of property
description: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any movable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than six months but which may extend to two years and with fine. Illustrations. ( a ) A takes property belonging to Z out of Z’s possession, in good faith believing at the time when he takes it, that the property belongs to himself. A is not guilty of theft; but if A, after discovering his mistake, dishonestly appropriates the property to his own use, he is guilty of an offence under this section. ( b ) A, being on friendly terms with Z, goes into Z’s library in Z’s absence, and takes away a book without Z’s express consent. Here, if A was under the impression that he had Z’s implied consent to take the book for the purpose of reading it, A has not committed theft. But, if A afterwards sells the book for his own benefit, he is guilty of an offence under this section. ( c ) A and B, being, joint owners of a horse. A takes the horse out of B’s possession, intending to use it. Here, as A has a right to use the horse, he does not dishonestly misappropriate it. But, if A sells the horse and appropriates the whole proceeds to his own use, he is guilty of an offence under this section. Explanation 1.—A dishonest misappropriation for a time only is a misappropriation within the meaning of this section. Illustration. A finds a Government promissory note belonging to Z, bearing a blank endorsement. A, knowing that the note belongs to Z, pledges it with a banker as a security for a loan, intending at a future time to restore it to Z. A has committed an offence under this section. Explanation 2.—A person who finds property not in the possession of any other person, and takes such property for the purpose of protecting it for, or of restoring it to, the owner, does not take or misappropriate it dishonestly, and is not guilty of an offence; but he is guilty of the offence above defined, if he appropriates it to his own use, when he knows or has the means of discovering the owner, or before he has used reasonable means to discover and give notice to the owner and has kept the property a reasonable time to enable the owner to claim it. What are reasonable means or what is a reasonable time in such a case, is a question of fact. It is not necessary that the finder should know who is the owner of the property, or that any particular person is the owner of it; it is sufficient if, at the time of appropriating it, he does not believe it to be his own property, or in good faith believe that the real owner cannot be found. Illustrations. ( a ) A finds a rupee on the high road, not knowing to whom the rupee belongs, A picks up the rupee. Here A has not committed the offence defined in this section. ( b ) A finds a letter on the road, containing a bank-note. From the direction and contents of the letter he learns to whom the note belongs. He appropriates the note. He is guilty of an offence under this section. ( c ) A finds a cheque payable to bearer. He can form no conjecture as to the person who has lost the cheque. But the name of the person, who has drawn the cheque, appears. A appropriates the cheque without attempting to discover the owner. He is guilty of an offence under this section. ( d ) A sees Z drop his purse with money in it. A picks up the purse with the intention of restoring it to Z, but afterwards appropriates it to his own use. A has committed the offence under this section. ( e ) A finds a purse with money, not knowing to whom it belongs; he afterwards discovers that it belongs to Z, and appropriates it to his own use. A is guilty of an offence under this section. ( f ) A finds a valuable ring, not knowing to whom it belongs. A sells it immediately without attempting to discover the owner. A is guilty of an offence under this section.
summary: Dishonest misappropriation or conversion of any movable property is punishable with imprisonment of six months to two years and a fine, with detailed illustrations and explanations clarifying when the act is dishonest.
punishment: punishment with imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than six months but which may extend to two years and with fine
ingredients: dishonest misappropriation or conversion
movable property
to the offender's own use

description: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any property, knowing that such property was in the possession of a deceased person at the time of that person’s decease, and has not since been in the possession of any person legally entitled to such possession, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine, and if the offender at the time of such person’s decease was employed by him as a clerk or servant, the imprisonment may extend to seven years. Illustration. Z dies in possession of furniture and money. His servant A, before the money comes into the possession of any person entitled to such possession, dishonestly misappropriates it. A has committed the offence defined in this section.
summary: Dishonest misappropriation of a deceased person's property, when not in possession of any legal heir, is punishable up to three years, extending to seven years if the offender was the deceased's clerk or servant.
punishment: punishment with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine, and if the offender at the time of such person’s decease was employed by him as a clerk or servant, the imprisonment may extend to seven years
ingredients: dishonest misappropriation or conversion
property of a deceased person
property not in possession of any legal heir
knowledge of the deceased's possession
explanation: Illustration. Z dies in possession of furniture and money. His servant A, before the money comes into the possession of any person entitled to such possession, dishonestly misappropriates it. A has committed the offence defined in this section.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 315, Page 84

title: Of criminal breach of trust
description: ( 1 ) Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits criminal breach of trust. Explanation 1.—A dishonest misappropriation for a time only is a misappropriation within the meaning of this section. Illustration. A finds a Government promissory note belonging to Z, bearing a blank endorsement. A, knowing that the note belongs to Z, pledges it with a banker as a security for a loan, intending at a future time to restore it to Z. A has committed an offence under this section. Explanation 2.—A person who finds property not in the possession of any other person, and takes such property for the purpose of protecting it for, or of restoring it to, the owner, does not take or misappropriate it dishonestly, and is not guilty of an offence; but he is guilty of the offence above defined, if he appropriates it to his own use, when he knows or has the means of discovering the owner, or before he has used reasonable means to discover and give notice to the owner and has kept the property a reasonable time to enable the owner to claim it. What are reasonable means or what is a reasonable time in such a case, is a question of fact. It is not necessary that the finder should know who is the owner of the property, or that any particular person is the owner of it; it is sufficient if, at the time of appropriating it, he does not believe it to be his own property, or in good faith believe that the real owner cannot be found. Illustrations. ( a ) A finds a rupee on the high road, not knowing to whom the rupee belongs, A picks up the rupee. Here A has not committed the offence defined in this section. ( b ) A finds a letter on the road, containing a bank-note. From the direction and contents of the letter he learns to whom the note belongs. He appropriates the note. He is guilty of an offence under this section. ( c ) A finds a cheque payable to bearer. He can form no conjecture as to the person who has lost the cheque. But the name of the person, who has drawn the cheque, appears. A appropriates the cheque without attempting to discover the owner. He is guilty of an offence under this section.
summary: Any person entrusted with property who dishonestly misappropriates, converts, or uses it against legal directions commits criminal breach of trust, with detailed explanations and illustrations clarifying dishonest intent.
ingredients: entrustment of property or dominion over property
dishonest misappropriation, conversion, or use
violation of legal direction or contract governing the trust
explanation: Explanation 1.—A dishonest misappropriation for a time only is a misappropriation within the meaning of this section. Illustration. A finds a Government promissory note belonging to Z, bearing a blank endorsement. A, knowing that the note belongs to Z, pledges it with a banker as a security for a loan, intending at a future time to restore it to Z. A has committed an offence under this section. But the name of the person, who has drawn the cheque, appears. A appropriates the cheque without attempting to discover the owner. He is guilty of an offence under this section.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 316, Page 84

description:
( 1 ) Property, the possession whereof has been transferred by theft or extortion or robbery or cheating, and property which has been criminally misappropriated or in respect of which criminal breach of trust has been committed, is designated as stolen property, whether the transfer has been made, or the misappropriation or breach of trust has been committed, within or without India, but, if such property subsequently comes into the possession of a person legally entitled to the possession thereof, it then ceases to be stolen property.
( 2 ) Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
( 3 ) Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, the possession whereof he knows or has reason to believe to have been transferred by the commission of Stolen property.
summary: Defines stolen property and criminalizes dishonest receipt or retention of such property, prescribing imprisonment up to three years, a fine, or both.
punishment: imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both
ingredients: property transferred by theft, extortion, robbery or cheating, or misappropriated
dishonestly receives or retains the stolen property
knows or has reason to believe it is stolen
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (No. 45 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 317, Page 85

title: Cheating
description: (1) Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to cheat. Explanation. —A dishonest concealment of facts is a deception within the meaning of this section. Illustrations. (a) A, by falsely pretending to be in the Civil Service, intentionally deceives Z, and thus dishonestly induces Z to let him have on credit goods for which he does not mean to pay. A cheats. (b) A, by putting a counterfeit mark on an article, intentionally deceives Z into a belief that this article was made by a certain celebrated manufacturer, and thus dishonestly induces Z to buy and pay for the article. A cheats. (c) A, by exhibiting to Z a false sample of an article intentionally deceives Z into believing that the article corresponds with the sample, and thereby dishonestly induces Z to buy and pay for the article. A cheats. (d) A, by tendering in payment for an article a bill on a house with which A keeps no money, and by which A expects that the bill will be dishonoured, intentionally deceives Z, and thereby dishonestly induces Z to deliver the article, intending not to pay for it. A cheats. (e) A, by pledging as diamonds articles which he knows are not diamonds, intentionally deceives Z, and thereby dishonestly induces Z to lend money. A cheats. (f) A intentionally deceives Z into a belief that A means to repay any money that Z may lend to him and thereby dishonestly induces Z to lend him money, A not intending to repay it. A cheats. (g) A intentionally deceives Z into a belief that A means to deliver to Z a certain quantity of indigo plant which he does not intend to deliver, and thereby dishonestly induces Z to advance money upon the faith of such delivery. A cheats; but if A, at the time of obtaining the money, intends to deliver the indigo plant, and afterwards breaks his contract and does not deliver it, he does not cheat, but is liable only to a civil action for breach of contract. (h) A intentionally deceives Z into a belief that A has performed A’s part of a contract made with Z, which he has not performed, and thereby dishonestly induces Z to pay money. A cheats. (i) A sells and conveys an estate to B. A, knowing that in consequence of such sale he has no right to the property, sells or mortgages the same to Z, without disclosing the fact of the previous sale and conveyance to B, and receives the purchase or mortgage money from Z. A cheats. (2) Whoever cheats shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both. (3) Whoever cheats with the knowledge that he is likely thereby to cause wrongful loss to a person whose interest in the transaction to which the cheating relates, he was bound, either by law, or by a legal contract, to protect, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both. (4) Whoever cheats and thereby dishonestly induces the person deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to make, alter or destroy the whole or any part of a valuable security, or anything which is signed or sealed, and which is capable of being converted into a valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
summary: Cheating is defined as deceiving a person to induce delivery of property or consent to retain property, or to cause an act or omission that results in damage. The offence includes various forms such as false pretences, personation, and concealment of prior sale. Punishment ranges from up to three years to up to seven years depending on the nature and consequences of the cheating.
punishment: Whoever cheats shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
ingredients: deceiving any person
fraudulently or dishonestly inducing the person to deliver any property or consent to retain property
intentionally inducing the person to do or omit an act which he would not otherwise do
act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to the person in body, mind, reputation or property
explanation: Explanation. —A dishonest concealment of facts is a deception within the meaning of this section. Illustrations. (a) A, by falsely pretending to be in the Civil Service, intentionally deceives Z, and thus dishonestly induces Z to let him have on credit goods for which he does not mean to pay. A cheats. (b) A, by putting a counterfeit mark on an article, intentionally deceives Z into a belief that this article was made by a certain celebrated manufacturer, and thus dishonestly induces Z to buy and pay for the article. (c) A, by exhibiting to Z a false sample of an article intentionally deceives Z into believing that the article corresponds with the sample, and thereby dishonestly induces Z to buy and pay for the article. A cheats. (e) A, by pledging as diamonds articles which he knows are not diamonds, intentionally deceives Z, and thereby dishonestly induces Z to lend money. A cheats. (f) A intentionally deceives Z into a belief that A means to repay any money that Z may lend to him and thereby dishonestly induces Z to lend him money, A not intending to repay it. A cheats.
